PL is not just another feature; it represents a major leap forward in how customers can operate, collaborate, and scale. It’s a new way of thinking about simultaneous project development and allows to manage exploration of new features and production line in parallel without compromising quality or focus.

The PL concept is designed to further increase the addressable market for SaaS ERP and allows customers to handle the increasing complexity of scaling in today’s fast-paced market. By enabling customers to run projects in parallel in an encapsulated tenant, we give flexibility to better address the diverse needs of our customers and help them rapidly respond to new opportunities as they arise.

The Parallel Line is available for 3-system landscape customers and included in their subscription.

The Main line is used for the initial implementation and daily operations to define scope, set up the organizational structure, configure and test business processes and use them productively.

The Parallel line was intensively discussed and tested with selected customers during the Early Adoption phase, and 2 main use cases turned out to be most relevant for our customers:

  1. Make business configuration enhancements and test them safely in a separate environment. The workspace is copied, including all configuration. Once enhancements are completed, they can be merged back into the main line, e.g. for a rollout of new countries/regions or other larger business configuration enhancements.
  2. Leverage the project line as kind of a sandbox system, e.g. to test run data migrations.

 

Customers can initiate a parallel line provisioning via SAP4Me through the 'Branch' step (1). Branch is a function that is used to set up the parallel line and replicate the existing configuration of the main line to the parallel line. ‘Rebase’ (2) is a function that is used to send any configurations created in the main line to the parallel line after branch. To complete the cycle, ‘Merge’ (3) is a function that is used to send any business configuration changes from the parallel line to the main line into production, once ready.

For those eager to delve into more details, the Digital Library (accessible with any S-user) and Notes 3580675 and 3542536 provide comprehensive overviews of the parallel line.

This is an update on how far we’ve come. PL is designed to be the next catalyst for growth – allowing our customers to expand, scale, and innovate more rapidly than ever before.

 

Wrap Up

With the introduction of Parallel Line and the continued evolution of our 3-system landscape and extensibility features, we are poised for even more significant growth. But we aren’t just focused on scaling – we’re focused on ensuring that each innovation we introduce aligns with our core values of quality, reliability, and customer empowerment.

We are committed to continuing our journey with the same passion and drive that got us here in the first place. The road ahead is full of opportunities, and with the Parallel Line, we’re more equipped than ever to navigate it.

 

Final Thoughts

What started as a steady, linear growth trajectory has transformed into something far more dynamic and exciting. The innovations we’ve introduced over the past few years – from the 3-system landscape to extensibility concepts and now PL – have not only fueled our product’s growth but have also positioned us to lead in an increasingly competitive market. With PL, we are now ready to enter a new era of growth and innovation.

For those eager to read more, the following “How-To” Practitioner blogs have been released.

Parallel Line Branch via SAP for ME

Introduction to the Parallel Line - SAP Community

And further details can be found here: Set Up Your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal

Thanks for tuning in, looking forward to your thoughts

Bert

entry.title: Automatic Replenishment in SAP EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
In today's competitive supply chain landscape, operational efficiency and inventory accuracy are crucial to meeting customer expectations and reducing fulfillment costs. Automatic Replenishment in SAP EWM is a strategic tool that helps businesses maintain seamless warehouse operations by ensuring that picking locations are consistently stocked without manual intervention. By automating the movement of goods from reserve areas to fixed picking bins based on real-time stock levels, businesses can prevent order delays, optimize labor utilization, and reduce stockouts. In this blog, we’ll explore how automatic replenishment supports warehouse efficiency and demonstrates its functionality through a practical test scenario.

Let’s dive into the key configuration steps that make this process work effortlessly behind the scenes.

Configuration:

1. Activate Replenishment strategies in storage type

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM>Internal warehouse process> Replenish Control> Activate Replenishment strategies in storage type

2. Maintain Document types and item types for warehouse request

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM>Internal warehouse process> Replenish Control> Maintain Document/item Categories for replenishment

3.  Assign Fixed Bins to the products

T. Code : /N/SCWM/BINMAT

4. Stock Removal Strategies

4.1 Specify Storage type Search sequence for Stock removal

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Goods Issue Process>Strategies > Specify Storage type  search sequence for stock removal

4.2 Determine Storage type Search sequence for Stock Removal

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Goods Issue Process>Strategies > Determine Storage type  search sequence for stock removal

5. Warehouse Process Type

5.1 Define Warehouse Process Type

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Cross Process Settings>Warehouse Task >Define Warehouse Process type

5.2 Determine Warehouse Process Type

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Cross Process Settings>Warehouse Task >Determine Warehouse Process type

6. Maintain Product Master

T. Code: /N/SCWM/MAT1

Test Case:

In this test scenario, we will walk through a practical example to validate how Automatic Replenishment works in SAP EWM. The goal is to demonstrate that a Warehouse Task is automatically created when the stock in a bin drop below the predefined minimum quantity threshold.

We’ll simulate a real-life outbound process, starting from the creation of a Sales Order, triggering a pick from fixed bins, and observing how the system handles replenishment post-picking. Verify that the system automatically triggers replenishment when the stock in a fixed bin falls below the minimum quantity after a picking confirmation.

Pre-requisites:
1. Ensure Sufficient Stock is Maintained in the Reserve Area
Before testing replenishment, verify that adequate stock is available in the Reserve Area (HLR1). This stock will be used by the system to automatically replenish the Fixed Bin once the stock level falls below the defined minimum threshold. The replenishment warehouse task will move stock from the reserve area to the fixed bin to ensure seamless picking operations.

2. Ensure Sufficient Stock is Available for Order Picking

Verify that enough stock is available in the Fixed Bin to fulfill the outbound delivery. Once the picking is executed and confirmed, the system checks if the remaining quantity in the bin has fallen below the configured minimum quantity. If so, the follow-up replenishment process is automatically triggered, creating a warehouse task to replenish the fixed bin from the reserve area ensuring readiness for future picking demands.

3. Stock Removal Strategies are Preconfigured for Order Picking

As a prerequisite, ensure that appropriate Stock Removal Strategies are already configured in the system. These strategies determine how the system selects stock from storage bins during the picking process for outbound deliveries. Having this in place ensures that the system can efficiently identify and pick stock from the correct bins when processing the sales order.

Create a Sales Order (T-Code: VA01)

Sales Order is created with a quantity of 26 units. Each fixed bin holds 10 units, and the minimum quantity is configured as 5 units (as shown in Step 3). After the picking process, we expect the system to identify that the stock has dropped below the minimum threshold and automatically trigger a replenishment task from the reserve area.

Create an Outbound Delivery in S/4HANA and Distribute to EWM (T-Code: VL01N)

Generate the outbound delivery based on the sales order and distribute it to the EWM system.

Process the Outbound Delivery Order in EWM (T-Code: /N/SCWM/PRDO)

Access the outbound delivery order and create the picking warehouse tasks.

Create Warehouse Task:
 Outbound Delivery > Follow-on Functions > Warehouse Task

Three Warehouse Tasks have been Successfully Created for Picking. As expected, the system generated three warehouse tasks one for each fixed bin, each containing 10 units, to fulfill the 26-unit Sales Order. Upon confirmation of these tasks, the stock level in each bin is evaluated. Since the remaining quantity falls below the defined minimum threshold, the system is expected to automatically trigger three replenishment tasks from the reserve area.

Let’s now verify these replenishment tasks in the Warehouse Monitor. à Filter with Open Warehouse Task and Whse Process Type - 3010

Immediate Replenishment tasks have been automatically Created. The system has instantly generated replenishment tasks following the confirmation of picking tasks. The automatic creation of replenishment warehouse tasks is a result of the key configuration settings completed earlier such as activating automatic replenishment, defining the appropriate process type, and setting up effective stock removal strategies (as outlined in Steps 1, 4, and 5). These configurations work together to ensure seamless replenishment when fixed bin stock falls below the minimum threshold.

Before proceeding with the confirmation of these replenishment tasks, let’s first review the current stock levels in the fixed bins for the relevant products. This will help us validate that the system correctly identified the stock falling below the minimum quantity threshold and responded as expected.

Stock Status Before Replenishment Confirmation
One bin shows 4 units remaining, while the other two bins have disappeared from available stock, likely due to full depletion. Let’s confirm the replenishment tasks and verify the updated stock levels in the fixed bins.

Post Replenishment Task Confirmation
After confirming the replenishment tasks, all fixed bins are fully restocked and now ready for picking in upcoming outbound processes, as illustrated in the image below.

This example demonstrates how Automatic Replenishment functions in SAP EWM. When stock levels in the picking area fall below the defined minimum quantity, the system proactively creates replenishment tasks to transfer stock from the reserve area to the fixed bins. This ensures that stock is consistently available for picking, supporting uninterrupted outbound operations and enhancing overall warehouse efficiency. It's a powerful feature that helps businesses maintain optimal stock levels and streamline their fulfillment processes.

entry.title: SAP Collaborate ERP 2502 further integrates SAP S/4HANA Cloud with Microsoft Teams
entry.content:
The latest release continues SAP’s implementation of Collaborative ERP that we announced in 2022. Since then, we have rolled out new functionality to make it easier for business users to get their work done in Microsoft Teams while relying on the business data in SAP S/4HANA system. In the 2408 release, we added the ability to share information from SAP Fiori elements apps as a adaptive card-based loop component.

Now, we are bringing you proactive updates into your Microsoft Teams environment. The ability to see your tasks to-do’s and situations in the Teams feed allows you to instantly approve and take action without disrupting your workflow or forcing you to shift context into another app.

Approve your To-Do’s right in your Team’s feed

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition customers, a new integration of To-Dos in Microsoft Teams

This feature allows you to work directly within Microsoft Teams and execute actions right from your team environment.

Read the documentation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition 2502 to see all the details on the Microsoft Teams Integration: Notification for To Do’s

How will you use this new capability? We’re looking forward to your feedback!

entry.title: A journey from Netweaver 7.50 to S/4HANA - Part 2: What's Cooking?
entry.content:
Find part 1 here to learn more about our project in general.

Most of the activities mentioned in this article are related to what I called "Worldwide DEV" in the image used in part 1.

Creating Cookbooks

Based on the results from the latest S/4HANA Readiness Checks, we created a list of all 132 Simplification Item (SI) notes we had findings for and downloaded them as PDF files to our Google-Workspace. We wanted to have them available "locally" in order to try and get help from Google's Gemini KI with categorizing the SI's content.

We then assigned several SIs to each team member in our development team to read through them and summarize the gist in a "cookbook". For that, we concentrated on extracting information about how a finding should be fixed, taking note of available quick fixes and replacement options which already exist or may need to be created. Well aware that findings for which "no functional alternative available" were shown, would require involvement of our functional teams, we highlighted these SI separately and estimated (much) more time for fixing them.

In the case of "replace old code with this new code", we provided some examples which we put together with the help of a sandbox system converted to S/4HANA. Here is a random example how this looked like in a cookbook:

The idea behind the creation of the cookbooks is to provide a starting point for a developer fixing a program's S/4HANA Readiness findings during the manual code conversion and ideally make it unnecesary to read many SIs in detail.

Some preparatory activities in the NetWeaver Dev systems

As we are going to set up retrofit from the NW to the S/4HANA systems we used some available time to already create stand-alone Z* entities in our NetWeaver DEV-systems while waiting for the new S/4HANA DEV systems to become available. These objects are for example Z-copies of domains, data elements, structures, messages or even some function modules where we currently use standard objects from components like IS-Health which will no longer be available in S/4HANA (I had already published a blog post about those interesting findings way back in 2019!).

Speaking of which: anybody have some ideas how to replace memory IDs from IS-Health which will no longer exist in S/4HANA? It's one of the object types, I asked about in this question but haven't received any answers yet.

Tackling objects locked in open transports in the NetWeaver Dev system

After the future S/4HANA DEV system was created as a copy of the NetWeaver DEV system, we needed to take a closer look at objects locked in modifiable transports in the NetWeaver DEV system. As a result of the system copy, the objects were no longer locked to a transport but contained the unfinished - or even no longer needed -  changes applied in the NetWeaver DEV system. This could obviously cause issues if any of these objects required changes for S/4HANA and we didn't want to accidentally introduce potentially "half-baked" changes into what was going to eventually become our productive S/4HANA landscape!

We therefore had another preliminary task on our hands in the new DEV system before it got converted to S/4HANA: restore the earlier version of the impacted objects based on a list of open transports and the objects they contained. The restore was either accomplished by reverting back to the earlier version or by copying and pasting the code from the productive system.

Should any of these open changes be finished in the NetWeaver landscape, we'll get the new versions of the objects via retrofit into the S/4HANA world.

That's it for the 2nd leg of our journey to S/4HANA and it’s time for some rest around the campfire for our hikers!

Image generated with Gemini

Cheers

Bärbel

entry.title: Massive Creation of Version-Controlled Bill of Material (Version BOMs) in SAP PEO
entry.content:
Introduction

A bill of material (BOM) is a list of all components required in a process to make a product or assembly, together with the quantities required, the unit of measure, the description, the storage location, the production supply area, etc… The components are known as BOM items.

On a normal BOM, the different changes states are represented using date effectivity, so changes depend on dates. For BOMs with a change history, the change states are identified using the Engineering Change Numbers (ECN).

SAP Production Engineering and Operations (PEO) introduced a new version-controlled BOM concept, where different change states of the BOM are represented by versions. This means no need to work with ECN and date combinations. The main highlight of SAP PEO is then the use of Version BOMs instead of Normal or Classic BOMs.

Version BOMs cannot be created in the SAP S/4HANA classic GUI transaction codes (e.g. CS01). Instead, they must be created in the “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813). The BOM details and fields are exactly the same; the only difference between BOM structure of PEO and PP is the version number. 

The following table contains the main differences between classic and version-controlled BOMs:

Image 1. Main differences between Classic BOMs and Version BOMs

 

Management of Version-Controlled BOMs

SAP PEO provides a range of features for handing over complex product structures to manufacturing with version-controlled BOMs, which can be very useful for the usage in prototyping and new design implementation. With SAP PEO, three different BOMs can be used for the same finished product, based on the engineering requirements and process complexity:

  • Engineering BOM (EBOM)
  • Planning BOM (PBOM)
  • Shop Floor BOM or Manufacturing BOM (MBOM)

The classic production engineering, planning, and execution process is sequential. Production engineering starts after product design was completed. Production planning starts after release of MBOM and Shop Floor Routing. Production execution can start only after engineering, planning, and the procurement of components is completed. Sequential processing leads to long overall lead times, typical of PEO scenarios.

Usually, the first version BOM you create in an SAP PEO process is an EBOM. EBOM is the engineering bill of material which comes from the engineering department of the new product. We can create EBOM in SAP manually with snapshots or directly without snapshots in the “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813).

The EBOM can be first incorporated into a PBOM, which is then used to create the MBOM automatically. PBOMs can be used in production versions, which are used by MRP. Differentiating between PBOM and MBOM, and between Planning Routing and Shop Floor Routing, has the following advantages:

  1. It helps reduce lead time.
  2. It provides tools and a process to translate Phantom BOMs used in engineering and production planning into flat MBOMs.

Phantom BOMs allow the use of the same set of components in several parent BOMs, improving the consistency across different parent BOMs. When the parent material's BOM is exploded, the components in the main assembly and the components in any nested phantom assemblies are added to the flat component list of the MBOM. Phantoms can only be added to EBOMs and PBOMs. When converting the PBOM into a MBOM, the BOM gets flattened.

Image 2. BOM explosion with flattened phantom components

 

Maintain Bill of Material

As mentioned before, Version BOMs cannot be created using classic GUI BOM transaction codes. Version-controlled BOMs can be managed in the “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813). This app is not a PEO specific application but can be used for all BOM types in SAP S/4HANA.

With this app, you can display and manage all your BOMs. Using the available filters, you can find and view existing BOMs, drill down to the level of detail you need, and perform any necessary actions (such as delete BOMs, obsolete BOMs or assign change records to BOMs).

You can create new BOMs (whether classical, version-based, or alternative BOMs) and insert the relevant components along with the required data, including any attachments. This must be done one by one material, and the following options are available for version-controlled BOMs:

  • “Create Version BOM”: Create the initial version BOM for a material
  • “Create Version”: Create a new version of an existing version BOM
  • “Copy BOM”: Copy BOM components into a new initial version BOM for a material, to reduce manual maintenance
  • “Create Alternative”: Copy BOM components into a new initial version BOM for a material, with a new alternative BOM.

When adding components, you have the following options (not exhaustive):

  • You can add new lines one by one
  • You can use “Add Multiple Rows” button, which allows to add several rows info at the same time (10 lines)
  • You can also use existing lines as a template by using button “Copy”

Image 4. “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813), header data.

Image 5. “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813), BOM items.

Image 6. “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813), BOM details.

 

Migrate Classic BOMs to Version-Controlled BOMs

In case you are working with classic BOMs, and you would like to use your existing classic BOMs in SAP PEO, you can perform a one-time migration to convert your classic BOMs into version-controlled BOMs. PEO provides for that the migration report RMPE_MIGRATE_BOM. Please notice that this report is not designed for continuous migration of classic BOMs created in transaction CS01; the migration should be performed only once.

Image 7. Selection screen for classic BOMs migration report.

On the selection screen you can enter the BOMs which you want to convert to version-controlled BOMs. You can specify the target BOM as well, as in which do you want to create. And you have an option to delete the existing classic BOM after the creation of the Version BOM. Please notice that this migration report cannot be used for configurable materials.

More details are available on SAP Help Portal, on PEO Implementation Guide section.

 

Standard APIs for BOM Version Management

The below Remote-enabled Function Modules (RFC) are available and can be used for manipulation of version BOMs:

  • MPE_VERSNBOM_CREATE_INITIAL: Creates initial BOM version (first version)
  • MPE_VERSNBOM_CREATE_VERSION: Creates new BOM version (with reference)
  • MPE_VERSNBOM_UPDATE: Updates BOM version
  • MPE_VERSNBOM_READ: Reads BOM version
  • MPE_VERSNBOM_CHANGE_STATUS: Changes BOM version status

These APIs support BOM for both Unitized and Non-Unitized Materials, so the effectivity parameters for every BOM item are also supported. If these APIs were not available on your system, you can check SAP note 2855536 “Remote enabled Function Modules (RFC) for Manipulating Version Controlled Material Bill of Materials (BOM)”.

 

Customer Use Case for Massive Creation of Version BOMs

Customer works with version-controlled PBOMs and MBOMs. PBOMs are mainly used to flatten the phantom components. For some minor simple products, MBOMs can be directly created, but the standard procedure consists of first creating the PBOM, and later creating the MBOM by updating the PBOM on a Change Record (CR).

Customer manufactures highly engineered discrete products which contain hundreds of components, so requirement was to be able to create new version BOMs by importing the data from an Excel file. Thus, users could manage the BOM changes more quickly in Excel. As standard SAP functionalities do not offer this possibility, a custom program was developed.

Basic data requested by customer to be maintained at component level was item category (only stock items), item number, component, component quantity (always basic unit of measure), special procurement type, production supply area, and effectivity parameters (several values possible). The main key requirements to be considered were:

  1. Only creation of new versioned BOMs, not to change data on existing or just created BOMs.
  2. The new BOM versions are only created, but not released.
  3. The BOM release process to be manually done after the BOM creation, on “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813). This would also help as a double-check for the BOM upload.
  4. Keep component links with Operation Activities from the previous BOM version.

For the creation of the initial BOM version, that is, the first BOM version for the material, plant, BOM usage, and BOM alternative, the API MPE_VERSNBOM_CREATE_INITIAL was used. For the creation of a new BOM version, that is, the following successive version of existing version for the material, plant, BOM usage, and BOM alternative, the API MPE_VERSNBOM_CREATE_VERSION was used. In this latest case, the new BOM version is created with reference to an existing released BOM version. Let’s call it the Previous BOM version.

SAP PEO manages version-controlled routings, called Shop Floor Routings (SFR), that are not dependent on time and contain more level of detail. Every SFR operation can have one or multiple Operation Activities (OAs). OAs enable to define detailed work instructions for production operators without making scheduling and costing on operation level more complicated. You can also assign qualifications and buyoffs to OAs. In addition, you can create any-order groups (any sequence) or simultaneous groups (parallel).

Components can be assigned to the more detailed OA level. On “Component Assignment Workspace” of the SFR, users can perform component assignment to OAs in the produce segment for the currently selected MBOM and unassign components. The true challenge of this custom massive creation program was to keep the same component links with the OAs of the SFR, as per the Previous BOM version for still existing BOM items in the new version. This was a mandatory requirement for customer, as minor changes are normally done within the hundreds of components of the BOM, and if existing links are lost this would mean to manually re-assign all the components again.

Technically, the component links are maintained though the tables STPO (field STPO- STVKN_VERSN, inherited node number of BOM item) and STPO_LINKS. To maintain this link, you need the field BOM item node number (STPO-STLKN) to be imported for those BOM items for which you want to keep the same component links to OAs. Thus, you can assign by code the same value for fields STLKN, STPOZ, and STVKN in the “T_BOM_ITEMS_IN” structure, for the items with update type “U”. For the new items or those not keeping the links, the update type will be “I” and unique identifier will be required for field NEW_ITEM_IDENT.

 

Practical Example

Let’s review a practical example to verify how component links are kept. Originally the “Component Assignment Workspace” of the SFR for the assembled material is the following:

Image 8. Component assignation for existing BOM version as reference.

We create a new PBOM version for the assembled material, release it, assign a CR, update the new MBOM version, and release it. On the new PBOM version, we changed on Excel the quantity of one BOM item, the effectivity of another, and we have added a new component item. NOTE: These changes can also be performed on the PBOM of a phantom assembly component, and components will also be linked (by means of table STPO_LINKS).

With the new MBOM version, on the “Component Assignment Workspace” of the SFR for the assembled material we can notice the following:

  1. For those components with a change on the quantity, the assignation status is “Partially Assigned” (yellow).
  2. For those components with a change on the effectivity parameters, the assignation status is “Assigned Outside Effectivity” (red).
  3. For those components without any of above changes or not changed at all, the assignation status is “Assigned” (green).
  4. For the new components there is no assignation (no icon).

Image 9. Component assignation for new BOM version created.

 

Programs for Exporting/Importing Version BOMs to/from Excel

Initially, the standard exporting functionality provided by “Manage Bill of Materials” Fiori App (F1813) was used. But we found the following issues:

  • We were not able to include the header data on the exported Excel.
  • We couldn’t split the parameter effectivity data into several columns.
  • We couldn’t split the parameter effectivity data into several lines, in case multiple effectivity values exist.

Image 10. Exporting BOM items on “Maintain Bill of Material” Fiori App (F1813).

These issues were causing users to have to manually change and adapt the layout for later data importing, thus reducing the process agility because of the different download and upload layouts. Therefore, customer decided to also create a new custom export program to export the existing BOM versions of materials in an Excel file, directly with the same agreed layout to be later used for importing and creating a new BOM version. Thus, users would make the required changes for the BOMs directly on the Excel file, without the need of changing any layout.

The flow chart of the final solution for massive exporting and importing version BOMs is the following:

Image 11. Flow chart of the final solution for massive exporting and importing version BOMs

The selection screen and output list of the new program to export version BOMs to Excel are displayed on below images:

Image 12. Selection screen for program to export version BOMs to Excel.

Image 13. Output list for program to export version BOMs to Excel.

Columns colored in orange are the ones to be exported to Excel, that is, the ones which have been agreed to later be imported from the Excel to create the new BOM version (agreed layout). Rest of columns are calculated fields (basically descriptions). The implemented stripped Zebra pattern uses the same color for all the lines belonging to the same material BOM version (same plant, material, usage, alternative, and BOM version), to provide a visual help for fast identification of the components of the same BOM.

The exportation to Excel format is a customized feature. We have a header row in bold grey and several rows with the data values. Key fields of the different BOM levels (header, item, item effectivity) are on italics. The layout to be shared by both programs is the following:

Image 14. Excel layout to be shared by both programs.

The selection screen and output list of the new program to import version BOMs from Excel are displayed on below images:

Image 15. Selection screen for program to import version BOMs from Excel.

Image 16. Output list for program to import version BOMs from Excel.

Before creation of the new BOM versions can take place, several verifications are done to assure the data imported is correct. Basically, the existence of the different objects (plant, assembled material, component, item category, special procurement type, production supply area, model) is verified, together with some other additional checks.

Both APIs MPE_VERSNBOM_CREATE_INITIAL and MPE_VERSNBOM_CREATE_VERSION used for new BOM version creation allow execution without commit, so there are two possible execution modes:

  • Test Mode: the BOM creation is simulated, and all the messages returned by the API are displayed on the execution log, but BOMs are not saved on the database.
  • Execution Mode: the BOMs are saved on the database.

With this program more than one version BOM can be uploaded each time, thus increasing the agility of the users working with BOM management. For the performance’s sake of the program, there is a limit of records on the imported Excel file, delimited by the field “Maximum Records”, which can be customized as desired.

After the execution process, the execution log is displayed, but it is also saved as a specific application log to be later monitored in case of issues.

Image 17. Execution log for program to import version BOMs from Excel.

Image 18. Application log for program to import version BOMs from Excel.

entry.title: RISE with Resilience: Google Cloud's Live Migration & Memory Poisoning Recovery
entry.content:
Downtimes: they're what keep us IT people awake at night—the absolute worst experience for any IT staff member. And what's the second worst thing? Trying to convince the business that we need a scheduled outage for maintenance. It's like asking permission to intentionally break something, so we can fix it better later.

As a SAP Customer Engineer at Google Cloud, I work closely with technologies that help ensure reliability for critical workloads. For organizations running mission-critical SAP workloads, maintaining uptime and ensuring business continuity is paramount.

Disruptions, both planned and unplanned, can lead to significant financial losses, damage customer trust, and create operational bottlenecks. SAP RISE streamlines the move to the cloud, but its effectiveness depends heavily on the cloud platform's reliability.  While SAP RISE provides a managed service with a default 99.7% System Availability SLA primarily addressing unplanned downtime, the underlying Google Cloud infrastructure includes features designed to further enhance real availability (planned + unplanned). For instance, Live Migration technology helps minimize planned maintenance downtime. Additionally, Google Cloud offers Memory Poisoning Recovery for its SAP HANA virtual machines, which, coupled with proactive monitoring capabilities, works to reduce the likelihood and impact of unplanned hardware-related outages.

Therefore, for SAP RISE customers, choosing a cloud platform with inherent high availability is a key business decision.

 

Live Migration: Planned Maintenance Without Business Interruption

Traditionally, routine hardware and hypervisor maintenance caused significant planned downtime in on-premises and other hyperscaler's infrastructure. Google Cloud's Live Migration changes this by allowing virtual machines, including those running critical SAP components like SAP S/4HANA and SAP HANA databases (up to 12TB), to be moved dynamically between servers without any perceptible interruption to SAP applications.

GCP Live Migration involves a phased transfer of a VM's state from a source to a target host within the same zone. Initially, memory is copied in a "brownout" phase while the VM continues running. A brief "blackout" pauses the VM for final state transfer, typically under a second, before resuming operation on the target host. This process preserves VM properties, including network configurations and storage attachments, without requiring a reboot, thus minimizing application disruption during hardware maintenance.

Here's why Live Migration is a game-changer for SAP RISE customers, partners, and SAP employees:

  • Eliminate Planned Downtime for Infrastructure Maintenance: Google Cloud can perform essential hypervisor and hardware maintenance, including critical security updates and infrastructure upgrades, without requiring any shutdown or reboot of the SAP systems. This translates directly to significantly higher uptime and a substantial reduction in disruptions for business users. 

  • Ensure Continuous Uptime for Mission-Critical SAP Servers: Live Migration guarantees that SAP applications remain consistently accessible, even during unforeseen underlying infrastructure events. Thus latest advancements in infrastructure technologies can easily be introduced by Google.

  • Enhanced Single Instance SLA: Google Cloud provides an industry-leading uptime SLA of 99.95% for single-zone (99.99% when deployed in multizones), memory-optimized VM instances, which are commonly utilized for SAP HANA deployments. Although SAP RISE offers the same default SLA under the RISE umbrella for the whole stack, this shows the confidence Google has for its technology.

Unlike other cloud providers who might offer Live Migration with considerable limitations or require VM restarts for certain maintenance activities , Google Cloud delivers comprehensive Live Migration capabilities across its SAP-certified virtual machine portfolio . This is a key differentiator that directly translates to tangible higher uptime for SAP RISE customers.

Memory Poisoning Recovery: A Proactive Defense Against Unplanned Hardware Errors

While Live Migration addresses planned downtime; unplanned hardware failures, particularly those stemming from memory errors, remain a potential risk in any infrastructure. In 2009, Google Cloud published the first major study on memory reliability. We found an average error rate of over 8% per year in DIMM modules installed in production systems. Given that each generation of DDR RAM packs more capacity into smaller packages, it's possible that memory hardware reliability has been impacted..

Google Cloud tackles this challenge head-on with its unique and proactive Memory Poisoning Recovery technology . It is a feature designed to mitigate the impact of uncorrectable memory errors. It works by:   

  • Detecting and isolating memory regions affected by these errors, preventing them from corrupting data.   
  • Employing techniques to minimize VM disruption, often using Google Cloud’s unique live migration feature to move VMs away from failing hardware.   
  • This system provides enhanced resiliency, especially for memory-intensive applications like SAP HANA, by reducing downtime associated with hardware memory failures.

Here's how Memory Poisoning Recovery fortifies SAP RISE deployments against unexpected disruptions:

  • Intelligent Detection of Uncorrectable Memory Errors: Memory Poisoning Recovery can detect uncorrectable memory errors on the underlying hardware infrastructure before they lead to system-wide failures.

  • Minimized Impact of Memory-Related Failures: If an uncorrectable memory error is detected, Google Cloud can perform a rapid restart of the affected VM while initiating a live migration of other VMs on the same server, preventing the propagation of failures across multiple SAP systems. This can also be coupled with SAP HANA Fast Restart to minimize impact on directly affected SAP HANA system. This feature is actively used by SAP Enterprise Cloud Services(ECS) for RISE deployments on Google Cloud

  • Significant Reduction in Downtime from Hardware Incidents: Memory Poisoning Recovery drastically reduces downtime from hardware failures, ensuring higher availability for business-critical SAP applications.

Google Cloud offers this proactive memory error recovery mechanism for all VM deployments free of charge, and it can be used for SAP HANA and Application virtual machines. This provides an unparalleled level of resilience for SAP RISE customers.

A Foundation of Unwavering Resilience for SAP RISE on Google Cloud

The strategic combination of Live Migration, which minimizes planned downtime to near zero , and Memory Poisoning Recovery, which provides a proactive defense against unplanned hardware errors, establishes a uniquely resilient infrastructure for SAP RISE deployments on Google Cloud.

By recommending and choosing SAP RISE on Google Cloud, SAP customers and partners can confidently embrace a cloud environment that’s engineered for continuous operations and minimal disruptions, like all other Google services we all enjoy every day! 

This allows organizations to decisively focus on driving innovation, achieving their strategic business objectives, and maximizing the value of their SAP investments with unwavering confidence in the platform's reliability.

entry.title: Product Inspiration Series - Experience the New Era of SAP CX
entry.content:
Dear Community,

We're happy to inform you that our Product Inspiration Series for Intelligent CX continues in 2025, with a new set of deep dive videos featuring most recent innovations from our SAP product teams and ISV partners. According to our AI-first and suite-first strategy those videos aim to inspire you on what's possible in the new era of AI agents and seamlessly connected end-to-end processes across SAP Business Suite. 

Thereby the special character of this video series remains unchanged. Each video...

  • covers specific innovations as part of the Intelligent CX portfolio,
  • is made from product or industry experts for experts,
  • typically starts with a short Q&A at the beginning (why, what, how),
  • does not contain any slides (zero slides rule),
  • consists of deep-dive and/or process demos,
  • wants to inspire you by showcasing real software,
  • is produced in 4K to offer a jaw-dropping viewing experience,
  • helps you understand the Consumer Industries & CX product strategy better, 
  • is made with love and dedication for you - our valued customers, partners, colleagues or simply CX enthusiasts.

But now let's have a closer look at the first videos which made it through the finish line...

1. Predictive Planning and Inventory Orchestration from SAP

2. Extensibility of SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 using No Code / Low Code Tools

3. Sinch Omnichannel Customer Engagement for SAP CX

4. Open Search Service for SAP Commerce Cloud in Action (coming soon)

5. Open Payment Framework for SAP Commerce Cloud in Action (coming soon)

6. Business Information Extraction for SAP Enterprise Service Management (coming soon)

 

7. Intelligent Selling with Joule in SAP Sales Cloud and S/4HANA Cloud in Action (coming soon)

If you have any questions or want to share your feedback please let me know. 

Further videos will be published soon. 🚀

Enjoy and have fun.

entry.title: Configuring SAP S/4HANA for Diverse Withholding Tax Scenarios
entry.content:
Withholding Tax (WHT), also known as Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) in some countries like India, is a crucial aspect of tax compliance. Businesses need to accurately deduct and remit taxes on payments made to vendors and other parties. SAP S/4HANA offers robust functionalities to configure and manage various withholding tax scenarios, ensuring adherence to legal requirements and streamlining tax processes.

In some jurisdictions, the obligation to deduct withholding tax arises at the earliest of two events:

  • Upon Credit: When the amount is credited to the partner’s account, encompassing capital accounts.
  • Upon Payment: When the payment is actually made to the partner, regardless of the method (cash, cheque, draft, or other means).

This blog post delves into different withholding tax scenarios, with a particular focus on the scenario where deduction occurs at the earlier of credit or payment, detailing the necessary customization steps for configuring these scenarios in SAP S/4HANA.

1. Formula Based withholding tax Calculation

In scenarios where withholding tax rates vary based on progressive slabs, SAP S/4HANA offers the formula-based withholding tax calculation feature.

Scenario: Calculate withholding tax based on the following annual income slabs:

  • Up to ₹ 3,000: 15%
  • ₹ 3,001 to ₹ 10,000: 22%
  • Above ₹ 10,001: 34%

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA:

A. Define withholding tax Code:

  • Navigate to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Calculation -> withholding tax Codes -> Define withholding tax Codes (or use transaction SPRO).
  • Create a new withholding tax code.
  • Crucially, activate the "W.tax form" flag (Technical field V_T059Z-XQFOR). This indicates that the calculation will be formula-based.

B. Define Formulas for Calculating withholding tax:

  • Go to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Calculation -> Define Formulas for Calculating withholding tax.
  • Create a new formula.
  • Enter the Validity date and Currency of the Company Code.
  • Define the slabs by entering the "Amount from", "Amount to", and the corresponding "Percentage". For the given scenario, you would define three steps in the formula. You can also define Reductions (optional) if applicable.

Amount from: 0, Amount to: 3000, Percentage: 15.

Amount from: 3001, Amount to: 10000, Percentage: 22

Amount from: 10001, (leave "Amount to" blank for infinity), Percentage: 34

System Behavior: When an invoice with this withholding tax code is processed:

  • The system will apply the defined formula to the withholding tax base amount.
  • It will calculate the withholding tax based on the progressive slabs defined in the formula.

 Example Calculation (Base Amount ₹ 12,000):

Using the defined formula, the calculation would be: (₹ 3,000 * 15%) + ((₹ 10,000 - ₹ 3,000) * 22%) + ((₹ 12,000 - ₹ 10,000) * 34%) = ₹ 450 + ₹ 1540 + ₹ 680 = ₹ 2670

 

2. TAN Based Exemption Limit Check (India Localization)

In India, businesses with a Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number (TAN) can obtain exemption certificates for certain vendors, allowing for withholding tax deduction only on the amount exceeding a specified limit. This exemption is often managed at the Permanent Account Number (PAN) level of the seller.

Scenario: withholding tax Section 194Q (withholding tax on purchase of goods) mandates withholding tax at 0.1% on the amount exceeding ₹ 50 lakh in a financial year from a seller if the total purchases from that seller exceed ₹ 50 lakh. This check needs to consider cumulative purchases across all vendors associated with the same seller PAN.

Configuration Steps in SAP ERP

A. Withholding tax Type for Invoice: Do not activate the standard accumulation functionality. Set the "Accumulation" to "No Accumulation".

B. Withholding tax Code for Invoice: Ensure that the "Calculation of withholding tax according to formula" checkbox is unchecked.

C. Mapping of Invoice and Payment WHT Types and Codes:

Map the Invoice time withholding tax type and code with the corresponding Payment time withholding tax type and code. This ensures consistency in exemption limit checks across both stages.

 

 

D. Maintain PAN in Vendor Master:

In the General Data view of the vendor master, under the "Vendor: Country-Spec. Enh." tab, maintain the PAN of the vendor.

E. Maintain TAN Exemption Data in Vendor Master (Company Code View):

Navigate to the Company Code view of the vendor master, under the "Vendor: Country-Spec. Enh." -> "India: TAN-Based Exemption" tab. Enter the following details:

  • Section code/ section code group (e.g., 194Q)
  • Certificate number issued by the tax authorities.
  • Exemption rate (the percentage of withholding tax exempted).
  • Exemption from and exemption to date (validity period of the certificate).
  • Reason for exemption.
  • Withholding tax code and tax type (for both invoice and payment).
  • Exemption threshold (e.g., 5000000 for ₹ 50 lakh). Ensure the same threshold is maintained for both invoice and payment types mapped to the same certificate number, even for duplicate vendors with the same certificate.
  • Currency.

System Behavior:

  • The system tracks the cumulative amount paid/credited across all vendors linked to the same PAN.
  • Once this cumulative amount exceeds the defined exemption threshold, withholding tax will be deducted on the excess amount for subsequent invoices or payments, considering the defined exemption rate.

 

3. Withholding Tax Grossing Up

Grossing up occurs when the payer (buyer) agrees to pay the seller (payee) a specific net amount and also covers the withholding tax liability on behalf of the seller. Essentially, the payer "grosses up" the payment so that after deducting the withholding tax, the seller receives the agreed-upon net amount.

In some countries, like Thailand, the concept of withholding tax grossing up is used. Here, the payer bears the withholding tax on behalf of the payee, resulting in the payee receiving the full invoice amount.

Scenario: A business needs to pay a vendor THB 1,000, and also pay the 5% withholding tax on the vendor's behalf.

Reason: This is often used in situations like:

  • Executive compensation or bonuses where the company wants the employee to receive a specific net amount.
  • Cross-border payments where the payer wants to ensure the foreign recipient receives the full intended amount without the burden of local withholding tax.

Accounting Impact: The payer accounts for the expense at a grossed-up value (net amount + withheld tax). The payment to the vendor is the agreed-upon net amount. The withheld tax is paid separately by the payer to the tax authorities, but it's treated as an additional cost/expense for the payer, not a deduction from the vendor's payment.

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA:

A. Define withholding tax Code:

Navigate to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Calculation -> withholding tax Codes -> Define withholding tax Codes.

For the relevant withholding tax code, maintain the "Posting indicator" value as "2". This setting signifies that the tax amount should be posted to a separate expense account instead of reducing the vendor payment.

B. Define Accounts for withholding tax for "Gross.Up" Offsetting Entry:

Navigate to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Posting -> Define Accounts for withholding tax. 

For the relevant withholding tax type and code, configure the "Expense account for withholding tax". This is where the grossed-up tax amount will be debited.

 

System Behavior: When an invoice subject to gross-up is posted:

  • The system will calculate the withholding tax amount.
  • The accounting entry will reflect the following (as per the example):
    • Debit: Business partner (full invoice amount - ₹ 1,000)
    • Debit: Withholding tax expense (grossed-up tax amount - ₹ 52.63)
    • Credit: Bank (total payment - ₹ 1,000)
    • Credit: Withholding tax payable (tax liability - ₹ 52.63)

The system effectively pays the vendor the full amount while also accounting for the withholding tax expense separately. The grossed-up amount for tax calculation is derived such that applying the WHT rate to it yields the desired tax amount. In the example, if the invoice is ₹ 1,000 and the WHT rate is 5%, the grossed-up amount is ₹ 1,052.63 (₹ 1,000 / (1 - 0.05)), and 5% of ₹ 1,052.63 is approximately ₹ 52.63.

 

Understanding Accumulation/Threshold Limit Checks

A fundamental concept in withholding tax is the accumulation/threshold limit check. This mechanism determines when withholding tax becomes applicable based on the total amount paid or credited to a supplier within a specific period. SAP S/4HANA, similar to SAP ECC, traditionally performs this check at the vendor master level.

Let's explore different variations of this check:

4. Accumulation Limit Check at Monthly Level

In certain scenarios, like rent payments, withholding tax might be applicable only when the monthly payment exceeds a specific threshold.

Scenario: Deduct withholding tax on rent payments only when the monthly rent exceeds ₹ 50,000.

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

A. Set Accumulation Type:

  • Navigate to the Define withholding tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key (e.g., IN for India).
  • Locate the withholding tax type relevant to your requirement (e.g.: Section 194I (Rent)).
  • Set the "Accumulation type" to "Per month". This instructs the system to track payments monthly for this WHT type.
  • Verify that the corresponding withholding tax code(s) for your withholding tax section (e.g. 194I) have the correct withholding tax rate defined.

B. Set Minimum Amount:

  • Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for withholding tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Find the withholding tax code associated with Section 194I.
  • Set the "Minimum base amount" to 50000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025 (assuming the new fiscal year starts then).

C. Vendor Master Assignment:

Ensure the relevant withholding tax code for 194I is assigned to the respective vendors in the Vendor Master data.

System Behaviour: When a rent invoice is entered for a vendor with this configuration:

  • The system will check the total rent paid to that vendor within the current month.
  • If the cumulative monthly rent exceeds ₹ 50,000, withholding tax will be calculated on the entire amount of the current invoice.

 

5. Accumulation Check at Transaction Level

In some cases, withholding tax applicability depends on the value of a single transaction, irrespective of the total amount paid to the vendor.

Scenario: Deduct withholding tax when a single transaction amount for lottery or game winnings is ₹ 10,000 or more (Section 194B).

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

A. Set No Accumulation:

  • Go to the Define withholding tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Find the withholding tax type for Section 194B.
  • Set the "Accumulation" field to "No accumulation". This ensures each transaction is evaluated independently.

B. Set Minimum Amount:

  • Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for withholding tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Locate the withholding tax code for Section 194B.
  • Set the "Minimum base amount" to 10000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025.

C. Vendor Master Linkage: Ensure the appropriate withholding tax code for 194B is linked to the relevant vendors in the Vendor Master.

System Behavior:

When a payment is made for lottery or game winnings:

  • The system will evaluate the amount of that specific payment.
  • If the single transaction amount is ₹ 10,000 or more, withholding tax will be deducted.

 

6. Accumulation Limit Check at Financial Year Level

With evolving tax regulations, new sections might be introduced with accumulation based on the financial year.

Scenario: Implement withholding tax under the newly introduced Section 194T, applicable to payments (remuneration, interest, commission) made by a firm to its partners, where withholding tax at 10% is levied if the total payment exceeds ₹ 20,000 in a financial year (starting April).

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

A. Define withholding tax Key:

  • Access the Define withholding tax Keys configuration activity (ID: 104229).
  • Create a new entry for 194T with a relevant description (e.g., "withholding tax on Partner Payments").

B. Define withholding tax Type:

  • Use the Define withholding tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Create a new withholding tax type for 194T.
  • Set the "Accumulation type" to "Per year starting with month: 4". (for Apr to Mar FY) This configures the system to track payments annually, starting from April.

C. Define withholding tax Code:

  • Create a new withholding tax code specifically for 194T.
  • Set the tax rate to 10%.
  • Assign the withholding tax key (194T) and the newly created withholding tax type to this code.

D. Set Minimum Amount:

  • Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for withholding tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Locate the withholding tax code for 194T.
  • Set the "Minimum base amount" to 20000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025.

E. Payment Posting WHT Type and Code (Optional): If required by your business processes, similarly create a corresponding withholding tax type and withholding tax code for payment postings related to Section 194T.

F. Vendor Master Assignment: In the Vendor Master for partners (treated as vendors), ensure the newly created withholding tax code(s) for 194T are added to their records.

System Behavior: When processing invoices or payments to partners:

  • The system will track the cumulative amount paid to that partner within the financial year (April to March).
  • Once the total exceeds ₹ 20,000, withholding tax at 10% will be deducted on the subsequent payments.

Conclusion

Configuring SAP S/4HANA for different withholding tax scenarios requires a thorough understanding of the specific legal requirements and careful implementation of the system's functionalities. By correctly setting up accumulation types, threshold limits, formula-based calculations, TAN-based exemptions, and gross-up mechanisms, businesses can ensure accurate withholding tax deductions, comply with regulations, and streamline their tax processes within the SAP environment. Remember to always test your configurations thoroughly in a non-production environment before deploying them in your live system.
